context:
Page 25, Section 6 (Concluding remarks), paragraph beginning “Limitation of the approach based on Volovikov’s theorem.”

However, it is still an open question whether or not a counterexample exists for some natural valuation functions.
title: Construct natural valuation counterexamples to envy-free multi-divisions with long knives
background_latex:
A multi-layered cake consists of \(m\) layers, each identified with the unit interval \([0,1]\). A \emph{layered piece} is an \(m\)-tuple \(L=(L_\ell)_{\ell\in[m]}\) where each \(L_\ell\subseteq[0,1]\) is a (finite union of) disjoint closed subintervals in layer \(\ell\). A \emph{multi-division} into \(q\) pieces is a \(q\)-tuple \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_q)\) of layered pieces whose interiors are pairwise disjoint and whose union covers the entire \(m\)-layered cake.

A layered piece \(L\) is \emph{contiguous} if each \(L_\ell\) is a single interval. It is \emph{non-overlapping} if for every two distinct layers \(\ell\neq \ell'\), the interiors of \(L_\ell\) and \(L_{\ell'}\) are disjoint as subsets of \([0,1]\) (equivalently, they intersect only on a finite set of points). A multi-division is \emph{feasible} if each \(A_j\) is non-overlapping, and it is \emph{contiguous} if each \(A_j\) is contiguous.

A common restricted class of feasible contiguous multi-divisions is given by \(q-1\) \emph{long knives}: one chooses cut positions \(0=t_0\le t_1\le\cdots\le t_{q-1}\le t_q=1\) and cuts \emph{every} layer at these positions, producing \(q\) intervals \([t_{r-1},t_r]\) in each layer. A feasible contiguous multi-division using \(q-1\) long knives then assigns to each bundle \(A_j\) exactly one of these \(q\) intervals from each layer, in such a way that within any fixed bundle \(A_j\), the chosen intervals from different layers have disjoint interiors in \([0,1]\).

Each agent \(i\in[n]\) has a \emph{valuation function} \(v_i\) that assigns a real value \(v_i(L)\) to every layered piece \(L\). (The paper discusses both general choice functions and valuations; here we focus on valuations as the paper’s open question does.) Envy-freeness for a multi-division \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_q)\) means there exists a surjective assignment \(\pi:[n]\to[q]\) such that every agent weakly prefers her assigned piece to any other, e.g. under valuations \(v_i(A_{\pi(i)})\ge \max_{j\in[q]} v_i(A_j)\) for all \(i\in[n]\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether there exist \emph{natural} valuation functions \(v_1,\dots,v_n\) (in the sense intended by the paper) for which \emph{no} envy-free multi-division exists that is both feasible and contiguous and is obtained using \(q-1\) long knives; equivalently, either:

1) construct an explicit instance (specified by \(m,n,q\) and valuations \(v_i\)) with this nonexistence property, or

2) prove that no such counterexample exists for a suitably broad and natural class of valuation functions.

The paper does not formalize what counts as “natural”; the task is to resolve existence of such counterexamples under an appropriate natural valuation-function model.

location_in_paper: Page 25, Section 6 (Concluding remarks), paragraph beginning “Limitation of the approach based on Volovikov’s theorem.”
open_problem_quote: However, it is still an open question whether or not a counterexample exists for some natural valuation functions.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can discretize valuations (e.g., piecewise-constant densities on a grid) and then brute-force over long-knife cut positions and bundle assignments to test envy-freeness, making automated search plausible. However, the space is still large and the paper does not specify what “natural” valuations mean, so search constraints may be ambiguous.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate counterexamples can be represented as parametrized piecewise-constant valuation densities across layers, with a fitness signal given by the minimum achievable envy (or infeasibility) over all long-knife divisions and assignments computed by optimization. The main challenge is the nested optimization (searching over divisions/assignments) and the ill-defined notion of “natural,” but iterative improvement could still produce informative hard instances.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The open question in Igarashi–Meunier concerns the gap between (i) strong prime-power existence theorems for envy-free feasible contiguous multi-divisions obtained by \(q-1\) long knives under very general preference/choice-function models, and (ii) known impossibility phenomena when \(q\) is not a prime power for general (possibly non-hungry) choice functions. The paper’s Theorem 1 shows that for \(m\le q\le n\) and \(q\) a prime power, one can always find an envy-free feasible contiguous multi-division using \(q-1\) long knives (indeed in a stronger “group-birthday” form), via a configuration-space/test-map scheme on the chessboard complex \(\Delta_{2q-1,q}\) and Volovikov’s equivariant Borsuk–Ulam-type theorem combined with Gale’s averaging trick. This establishes that within the long-knife restriction, prime-power \(q\) is sufficient for existence even without monotonicity or additivity assumptions.

On the negative side, the broader literature (notably Avvakumov–Karasev and Panina–Živaljević, building on the same equivariant-topological framework) shows that for non-prime-power \(q\) there are cake-cutting instances with choice functions for which envy-free divisions fail to exist. These counterexamples demonstrate a genuine topological obstruction once one allows sufficiently pathological preferences, and they explain why Volovikov-based proofs cannot extend beyond prime powers. However, these constructions do not immediately translate to standard “natural” valuation models (e.g., additive, absolutely continuous, monotone/Lipschitz), and Igarashi–Meunier explicitly leave open whether one can realize a nonexistence instance for envy-free feasible contiguous long-knife multi-divisions using such valuations.

Algorithmically, Deng–Qi–Saberi provide an FPTAS for approximate envy-free divisions in the classical one-layer, three-agent setting with monotone Lipschitz valuations; Igarashi–Meunier extend this style of monotonicity-exploiting search to a two-layer setting with one short and one long knife (Theorem 3). These results suggest that for several standard valuation classes, envy-free (or approximately envy-free) divisions are robustly attainable under structured knife models, reinforcing the plausibility that valuation-based counterexamples—if they exist—may require carefully engineered non-additive or non-monotone features. At present, with no forward-citation evidence of a valuation-based impossibility construction, the problem remains open: either one must build explicit valuations that defeat all long-knife feasible contiguous allocations, or prove a general existence theorem for a broad valuation class that bypasses the prime-power barrier known for choice functions.

sources.title: Envy-Free Division of Multi-Layered Cakes
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves existence of envy-free feasible contiguous multi-divisions using \(q-1\) long knives when \(q\) is a prime power (under closed preferences), and shows nonexistence for non-prime-power \(q\) under general choice functions via known counterexamples; explicitly leaves open whether counterexamples exist for natural valuation functions.
sources.connection: This is the source paper that formulates the open question: whether, under natural valuation models (as opposed to arbitrary choice functions), there are instances with no envy-free feasible contiguous multi-division obtainable by \(q-1\) long knives.

sources.title: Envy-free division using mapping degree
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes existence of envy-free cake divisions for \(n\) a prime power without hunger assumptions using mapping degree; also provides nonexistence phenomena for non-prime-power \(n\) in the choice-function setting.
sources.connection: Provides the prime-power barrier and the type of counterexamples (for choice functions) that motivate asking whether similar impossibility can be realized by natural valuation functions in the multilayer/long-knife model.

sources.title: Splitting necklaces, with constraints
sources.theorem_or_result: Applies Volovikov-type equivariant topology and configuration-space methods to constrained fair division (necklace splitting), giving tools and templates for prime-power existence results.
sources.connection: Methodological precursor: the multilayer long-knife existence proof uses the same Volovikov framework; understanding its limits helps assess whether valuation-based counterexamples might exist beyond prime powers.

sources.title: Envy-free division via configuration spaces
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops configuration-space/test-map schemes for envy-free division with non-hungry preferences, refining Volovikov-based approaches and discussing failures for non-prime-power group actions.
sources.connection: Clarifies the topological obstruction landscape underlying the long-knife model and why non-prime-power cases admit choice-function counterexamples; informs attempts to translate such obstructions to valuation models.

sources.title: Fair division of time: Multi-layered cake cutting
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces the multi-layered cake model with non-overlap feasibility; proves existence of envy-free feasible divisions when contiguity is relaxed and resolves small cases (e.g., \(m=2,n=2\)) with long knives; leaves open contiguous feasible envy-free existence for \(m=2,n=3\) and more generally.
sources.connection: Original formulation of the multilayer feasibility constraint and the long-knife restriction; the open problem asks for valuation-based impossibility instances for envy-free feasible contiguous long-knife divisions.

sources.title: Algorithmic solutions for envy-free cake cutting
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives an FPTAS for \(\varepsilon\)-envy-free contiguous cake cutting for three agents with monotone Lipschitz valuations; establishes computational hardness for more general preference representations.
sources.connection: Algorithmic baseline extended in the source paper to two-layer settings; relevant for understanding what 'natural' valuation classes (monotone, Lipschitz, additive) support constructive envy-free divisions under knife restrictions.
